examples.title: Cloud Resource Provisioning Process
examples.description: Demonstrates the step-by-step process of provisioning a virtual machine in the cloud
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ProvisionVM(userRequest)
BEGIN
  AUTHENTICATE_USER(userRequest.credentials)
  VALIDATE_QUOTA(userRequest.requiredResources)
  
  // Select appropriate hypervisor
  SELECT_HYPERVISOR(userRequest.osType)
  
  // Allocate resources from pool
  ALLOCATE_CPU(userRequest.cpuCores)
  ALLOCATE_MEMORY(userRequest.ramGB)
  ALLOCATE_STORAGE(userRequest.storageGB)
  
  // Configure networking
  CONFIGURE_NETWORK(userRequest.vpc, userRequest.subnet)
  
  // Create and start VM
  CREATE_VIRTUAL_MACHINE()
  ATTACH_STORAGE()
  ASSIGN_IP_ADDRESS()
  START_VM()
  
  // Start metering for billing
  INITIATE_METERING()
  
  RETURN vmInstance
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the cloud provisioning workflow: user authentication, resource validation, hypervisor selection, resource allocation from pool, VM creation, network configuration, and billing setup.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n = VM resources allocated

examples.title: Simple Auto Scaling Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates how cloud elasticity works by scaling resources based on demand
examples.code:
class CloudAutoScaler:
    def __init__(self, min_instances=1, max_instances=10, target_cpu=70):
        self.min_instances = min_instances
        self.max_instances = max_instances
        self.target_cpu = target_cpu
        self.current_instances = 2
    
    def check_load_and_scale(self, current_cpu_usage):
        print(f"Current instances: {self.current_instances}, CPU: {current_cpu}%")
        
        # Scale out if CPU above target
        if current_cpu_usage > self.target_cpu and self.current_instances < self.max_instances:
            self.current_instances += 1
            print(f"SCALING OUT: Added instance. Now running {self.current_instances} instances")
        
        # Scale in if CPU below threshold
        elif current_cpu_usage < (self.target_cpu - 20) and self.current_instances > self.min_instances:
            self.current_instances -= 1
            print(f"SCALING IN: Removed instance. Now running {self.current_instances} instances")
        
        return self.current_instances

# Simulation
scaler = CloudAutoScaler()
cpu_readings = [85, 90, 75, 60, 45, 30, 80, 95]

for cpu in cpu_readings:
    scaler.check_load_and_scale(cpu)
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates cloud auto-scaling, a key elasticity feature. When CPU usage exceeds target (70%), it adds instances (scale out); when below threshold, it removes instances (scale in).

examples.title: Load Balancer Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates basic round-robin load balancing across cloud servers
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates round-robin load balancing, distributing incoming requests across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed. In real cloud environments, more sophisticated algorithms (least connections, weighted) are used.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n = number of servers

examples.title: Cloud Billing Calculation
examples.description: Shows how cloud providers calculate costs based on resource consumption
examples.code:
PROCEDURE CalculateBill(usageMetrics)
BEGIN
  // Define rates (example)
  COMPUTE_RATE = 0.05  // per hour per instance
  STORAGE_RATE = 0.10  // per GB per month
  DATA_TRANSFER_RATE = 0.08  // per GB
  
  // Calculate compute charges
  computeCost = usageMetrics.instanceHours * COMPUTE_RATE
  
  // Calculate storage charges  
  storageCost = usageMetrics.storageGB * STORAGE_RATE
  
  // Calculate data transfer charges
  dataCost = usageMetrics.dataOutGB * DATA_TRANSFER_RATE
  
  // Apply discounts
  IF usageMetrics.prepaidHours > 100 THEN
    computeCost = computeCost * 0.90  // 10% discount
  ENDIF
  
  totalCost = computeCost + storageCost + dataCost
  
  // Generate bill
  PRINT "Compute: $" + computeCost
  PRINT "Storage: $" + storageCost
  PRINT "Data Transfer: $" + dataCost
  PRINT "Total: $" + totalCost
  
  RETURN totalCost
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates metered billing in cloud computing. Costs are calculated based on actual consumption (compute hours, storage used, data transferred) with potential discounts for commitments, illustrating the pay-as-you-go model.
